blocks.text: Anyone who claims to know something does not yet have the necessary knowledge;
but anyone who loves God is known by him.
Hence, as to the eating of food offered to idols, we know that “no idol in the world really exists,” and that “there is no God but one.”
4 : The Corinthians’ knowledge that is grounded in the monotheistic faith to which they have been converted; cf. Deut 4.35; 6.4; Wis 7.17; 13.1; 15.2–3.
Indeed, even though there may be so-called gods in heaven or on earth—as in fact there are many gods and many lords—
5 : Paul acknowledges not that exist, but that they are worshiped as such by others.
yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist.
6 : Paul quotes an early confession; cf. Rom 11.36; Col 1.15–16.

It is not everyone, however, who has this knowledge. Since some have become so accustomed to idols until now, they still think of the food they eat as food offered to an idol; and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.

7 : Paul confronts the “strong” with the reality that some do not share their enlightened attitude toward the gods. Those who have been must be Gentiles rather than Jews. The Greek word translated does not correspond to the modern conception of an internal guide to moral conduct in the future, but denotes a past misdeed which causes pain. The “strong” have asserted that their consumption of idol meat did not convict them of any wrongdoing, and have alleged that the of other Christians is for harboring such scruples. Paul appropriates the term from the “strong” and uses it to express his own true concern that someone for whom the (v. 5) have existence might be by eating .
“Food will not bring us close to God.” We are no worse off if we do not eat, and no better off if we do.
8 : The entire verse is another quotation from the Corinthians’ letter, despite the NRSV's punctuation.
But take care that this liberty of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to the weak.
9 : The Greek word translated has a meaning closer to “authority” or “right,” as in 9.4–6,12,18.
For if others see you, who possess knowledge, eating in the temple of an idol, might they not, since their conscience is weak, be encouraged to the point of eating food sacrificed to idols?
10 : The situation envisioned here is illustrated by the temple of Asclepius at Corinth, which had three dining rooms on the east side of the courtyard.
So by your knowledge those weak believers for whom Christ died are destroyed.
But when you thus sin against members of your family, and wound their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ.
11–12 : Paul formulates a radical conclusion regarding the relationship between and love: the proper object of religious concern is not an abstract truth about God, but .
Therefore, if food is a cause of their falling, I will never eat meat, so that I may not cause one of them to fall.
8.1–13: Eating food offered to idols.
13 : Paul formulates a general principle of Christian ethics: self-limiting regard for others.
